lessons.title: The Process Model: PIDs, Trees & Lifecycle
lessons.content:
Every running program is a **process** identified by a numeric **PID**. Each process (except the very first) has a **parent**, recorded as its **PPID**, forming a tree you can view with `pstree`. The root of that tree is **PID 1** — historically `init`, today almost always `systemd`. PID 1 is special: it is started directly by the kernel, it cannot be killed by ordinary signals, and it has the unique duty of **adopting orphans** and reaping them.

New processes are born by **`fork()`**, which clones the caller, then usually **`exec()`**, which replaces the clone's program image while keeping the PID. The pair is the universal "start a new program" pattern on Linux. A process ends with **`exit()`**, and its parent collects the exit status with **`wait()`**.

```bash
# Show a process and its ancestry
ps -o pid,ppid,stat,comm -p $$
pstree -p $$        # this shell back up to PID 1

# Watch fork/exec happen
strace -f -e trace=fork,clone,execve -- ls /tmp 2>&1 | head
```

If a parent dies before its child, the child is **reparented** to PID 1, which becomes responsible for reaping it. This is why a well-behaved PID 1 (or container init) *must* call `wait()` in a loop — something an ordinary process is never obligated to do. Understanding this fork/exec/exit/wait cycle is the foundation for everything else in this module: signals, job control, and how `systemd` supervises services.

items.front: What is a PID and a PPID?
items.back: PID is the unique numeric identifier of a process. PPID is the PID of that process's parent — the process that fork()ed it (or PID 1 after reparenting).
items.hint: One identifies the process, the other its creator.

items.front: What program is PID 1, and what makes it special?
items.back: PID 1 is init — today usually systemd. The kernel starts it directly, it adopts orphaned processes, it must reap them with wait(), and it cannot be terminated by ordinary signals.
items.hint: It sits at the root of the process tree.

items.front: Describe the fork/exec pattern.
items.back: fork() clones the calling process (same PID kept by parent, new PID for child). The child then calls exec() to replace its program image while keeping its PID. Together they launch a new program.
items.hint: Clone first, then replace the image.

items.front: What happens to a child whose parent dies first?
items.back: It becomes an orphan and is reparented to PID 1 (init/systemd), which then becomes responsible for reaping it via wait().
items.hint: Adoption by the root of the tree.

items.front: What does wait() do?
items.back: It lets a parent collect the exit status of a terminated child, which also removes the child's entry from the process table (reaping it).
items.hint: How a parent learns its child died.

items.prompt: Which command best displays the parent/child ancestry of processes as a tree?
items.explanation: pstree renders the process hierarchy. free shows memory, uptime shows load, and lsof lists open files — none show the tree.

items.prompt: After a successful fork() followed by exec() in the child, what is true of the child's PID?
choices: It changes to a new value during exec()
It stays the same as the PID assigned at fork()
It always equals the parent's PID
items.explanation: exec() replaces the program image but preserves the PID. The new PID is assigned at fork() and does not change when the image is replaced.

items.prompt: What duty must PID 1 perform that ordinary processes need not?
choices: Reap orphaned/adopted children by calling wait()
Run with the highest CPU priority
items.explanation: PID 1 adopts orphans and must reap them in a wait() loop, or the system fills with zombies. Ordinary processes only reap their own children.

lessons.title: Process States & Viewing Tools
lessons.content:
At any instant a process is in one **state**, shown in the `STAT` column of `ps`:

- **R** — *Running or runnable* (on a CPU or waiting for one).
- **S** — *Interruptible sleep*: blocked but **signalable** (most idle processes).
- **D** — *Uninterruptible sleep*: blocked deep in the kernel, usually on disk or NFS I/O.
- **Z** — *Zombie*: finished, but the parent hasn't `wait()`ed yet.
- **T** — *Stopped*: paused by `SIGSTOP`/`SIGTSTP` (e.g. Ctrl-Z).

The one to internalize is **D**. A D-state process is parked inside a kernel call that cannot be safely interrupted, so it does **not** check for signals — **even `SIGKILL` is ignored** until the I/O returns. That's why `kill -9` sometimes "does nothing": the process literally cannot run any code, including a death, until the kernel operation completes. Fix the I/O (the stuck disk, the dead NFS mount), not the signal.

```bash
# BSD vs SysV syntax both work:
ps aux                 # BSD style, every process
ps -ef                 # SysV style, full listing
ps -eo pid,stat,wchan,comm   # see WHY a process sleeps

top                    # live view; press 'x' to sort, 'f' fields
pgrep -a sshd          # PIDs (and cmdline) matching a name
pkill -TERM nginx      # signal by name pattern
```

`top`/`htop` give a live, sortable view; `pgrep`/`pkill` let you target processes by name instead of hunting PIDs. The `wchan` column tells you the kernel function a sleeping process is parked in — invaluable for diagnosing a stubborn D-state.

items.front: What do the process states R, S, D, Z, T mean?
items.back: R = running/runnable; S = interruptible sleep (signalable); D = uninterruptible sleep (kernel I/O, not signalable); Z = zombie (exited, not yet reaped); T = stopped (paused by SIGSTOP/SIGTSTP).
items.hint: Five letters in the ps STAT column.

items.front: Why won't a process in D-state die even with kill -9?
items.back: D is uninterruptible sleep: the process is blocked inside a kernel operation (usually I/O) that can't be safely interrupted, so it doesn't check pending signals — including SIGKILL — until the I/O completes.
items.hint: It can't run any code, not even its own death.

items.front: How do you actually clear a stuck D-state process?
items.back: You can't signal it away. Resolve the underlying I/O: recover the hung disk, restore or force-unmount the dead NFS share, etc. Once the kernel call returns, the process can act on its pending signals.
items.hint: Fix the cause, not the symptom.

items.front: Contrast ps aux with ps -ef.
items.back: Both list all processes. 'ps aux' is BSD-style syntax (no dash, shows %CPU/%MEM/START). 'ps -ef' is SysV-style (dash, shows UID/PPID/full command). Same data, different conventions.
items.hint: One has a dash, one doesn't.

items.front: What do pgrep and pkill do?
items.back: They match processes by name/attributes. pgrep prints matching PIDs (with -a, the full command line); pkill sends a signal to all matches — no need to look up PIDs manually.
items.hint: grep and kill, but for processes by name.

items.prompt: A process shows STAT 'Z'. What does this indicate?
choices: It has exited but its parent has not yet called wait() to reap it
It is swapped entirely to disk
It is waiting on a network socket
items.explanation: Z (zombie/defunct) means the process finished and is holding only a process-table slot for its exit status, pending a wait() from its parent.

items.prompt: You run 'kill -9 <pid>' on a process stuck in D-state and nothing happens. Why?
choices: SIGKILL is being caught by a handler
The process is in uninterruptible sleep and cannot process the signal until its kernel I/O returns
You lack permission to signal it
items.explanation: In D-state the process is blocked in an uninterruptible kernel operation and cannot check pending signals — SIGKILL queues but is not delivered until the I/O completes. SIGKILL itself is never catchable.

items.prompt: Which ps field helps you see WHY a sleeping process is blocked?
items.explanation: wchan shows the kernel function the process is sleeping in, which reveals what it's waiting on (e.g. an I/O routine) — key for diagnosing D-state hangs.

items.prompt: Which tool gives a continuously updating, sortable view of running processes?
items.explanation: top (or htop) refreshes live and lets you sort by CPU, memory, etc. ps is a one-shot snapshot, pstree shows hierarchy, nohup detaches a job from the terminal.

items.front: Process state transition diagram (R/S/D/Z/T)
items.back: State diagram showing transitions between R (running), S (interruptible sleep), D (uninterruptible sleep), T (stopped), and Z (zombie), with the signals and events that trigger each transition.

lessons.title: Signals: Delivery, Disposition & trap
lessons.content:
A **signal** is an asynchronous notification the kernel delivers to a process. Each has a default **disposition** (action) the program can usually override by installing a handler. Common ones:

- **SIGTERM (15)** — polite "please terminate," the default of `kill`. Catchable, so programs can clean up.
- **SIGINT (2)** — Ctrl-C interrupt from the terminal.
- **SIGHUP (1)** — "hangup"; historically terminal closed, now widely reused to mean "reload config."
- **SIGSTOP / SIGCONT** — pause and resume a process.
- **SIGKILL (9)** — immediate, forcible termination.

Two signals are **uncatchable, unblockable, and unignorable**: **SIGKILL** and **SIGSTOP**. The kernel acts on them *for* the process — it never hands them to user code. This is deliberate: SIGKILL is the guaranteed last resort so a buggy or malicious program can't trap its own kill and refuse to die. The flip side is that SIGKILL gives the process **no chance to clean up** (flush buffers, remove lock files), which is why you try SIGTERM first.

```bash
kill -TERM 4242        # ask nicely (default)
kill -9 4242           # SIGKILL: cannot be trapped, no cleanup
kill -l                # list all signal names/numbers

# Trap SIGTERM/SIGINT in a script to clean up on exit
cleanup() { rm -f /tmp/work.$$; echo "cleaned up"; }
trap cleanup TERM INT EXIT
while true; do sleep 1; done
```

In shell scripts, **`trap`** installs handlers for catchable signals — ideal for removing temp files or stopping children on `SIGTERM`. But you can never `trap` SIGKILL or SIGSTOP; the shell will accept the syntax for KILL but it will simply never fire. Design cleanup around SIGTERM and accept that SIGKILL means abrupt death.

items.front: Difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL?
items.back: SIGTERM (15) is a catchable, polite request to terminate — the process can clean up first. SIGKILL (9) is forcible and uncatchable — the kernel kills the process immediately with no cleanup. Always try TERM before KILL.
items.hint: One can be handled, one cannot.

items.front: Which two signals cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored?
items.back: SIGKILL and SIGSTOP. The kernel enforces them directly without ever delivering them to user code.
items.hint: 9 and the one that pauses you.

items.front: Why is SIGKILL deliberately uncatchable?
items.back: So there is always a guaranteed way to terminate a process. If programs could trap SIGKILL, a buggy or malicious one could refuse to die. The cost is no cleanup chance, so SIGTERM is tried first.
items.hint: It's the last resort that must always work.

items.front: What does SIGHUP traditionally mean, and how is it commonly reused?
items.back: Originally 'hangup' — the controlling terminal closed. Many daemons now repurpose SIGHUP to mean 'reload your configuration without restarting.'
items.hint: Hangup, but daemons read it as 'reload'.

items.front: What does the shell 'trap' builtin do?
items.back: It installs a handler that runs when the script receives a specified catchable signal (or pseudo-events like EXIT) — commonly used to clean up temp files or child processes on SIGTERM/SIGINT.
items.hint: Run my cleanup when this signal arrives.

items.prompt: A colleague writes 'trap cleanup KILL' in a bash script. What actually happens when the process receives SIGKILL?
choices: cleanup runs, then the process exits
cleanup never runs; the kernel terminates the process immediately
The signal is converted to SIGTERM
items.explanation: SIGKILL can never be trapped. The trap is silently ineffective — the kernel kills the process without running any handler.

items.prompt: Which signal does 'kill <pid>' send when no signal is specified?
items.explanation: Plain kill defaults to SIGTERM (15), the catchable request-to-terminate, giving the process a chance to shut down cleanly.

items.prompt: Pressing Ctrl-C in a terminal sends which signal to the foreground process?
items.explanation: Ctrl-C sends SIGINT (interrupt). Ctrl-Z would send SIGTSTP (stop). SIGINT is catchable, so programs can intercept it.

items.prompt: Why should you prefer SIGTERM over SIGKILL when stopping a service?
choices: SIGTERM lets the process run cleanup (flush buffers, remove locks) before exiting
items.explanation: SIGTERM is catchable, so a well-written program can flush data and release resources. SIGKILL gives no such opportunity and risks corruption or stale lock files.

lessons.title: Job Control, Priorities & Resource Limits
lessons.content:
Your shell groups the processes it launches into **jobs** and arranges them into **process groups** and a **session** — the machinery behind terminal job control.

- Append **`&`** to run a command in the **background**.
- **`Ctrl-Z`** sends **SIGTSTP**, stopping the foreground job (state T).
- **`jobs`** lists them; **`fg %1`** resumes job 1 in the foreground, **`bg %1`** resumes it in the background (sends SIGCONT).
- **`disown`** removes a job from the shell's table so it won't get SIGHUP when the shell exits.
- **`nohup cmd &`** runs a command immune to SIGHUP, redirecting output to `nohup.out`.
- **`setsid`** starts a command in a brand-new session, fully detached.

```bash
sleep 1000 &          # background, prints [1] 12345
jobs -l               # list jobs with PIDs
fg %1                 # bring it forward; Ctrl-Z to stop again
bg %1                 # resume in background
disown %1             # survive shell logout
nohup ./backup.sh &   # immune to hangup, logs to nohup.out
```

**Priority:** the **niceness** value (−20 to +19, default 0) hints the scheduler. *Higher* nice = *lower* priority (you're being "nice" to others). Start a job nicer with `nice -n 10 cmd`; change a running one with `renice 5 -p <pid>`. Only root can set negative (higher-priority) values.

```bash
nice -n 15 tar -czf big.tgz /data   # low-priority background work
renice -n -5 -p 12345               # needs root: raise priority
ulimit -a                            # show current resource limits
ulimit -n 4096                       # raise open-file-descriptor cap
```

**Resource limits** are per-process ceilings (`getrlimit`/`setrlimit`) you inspect and adjust with **`ulimit`**: max open files (`-n`), max processes (`-u`), core dump size (`-c`), memory. Soft limits can be raised up to the hard limit; only root raises the hard limit. These caps are inherited across `exec`, so a limit you set in a login shell shapes everything it launches.

items.front: What does appending '&' to a command do?
items.back: It runs the command in the background as a job, returning the prompt immediately. The shell prints the job number and PID, and the process keeps running while you do other things.
items.hint: Background the job.

items.front: What does Ctrl-Z do, and how do you resume the job?
items.back: Ctrl-Z sends SIGTSTP, stopping (pausing) the foreground job (state T). Resume it with 'fg' (foreground) or 'bg' (background), which delivers SIGCONT.
items.hint: Pause now; fg or bg to continue.

items.front: Difference between nohup and disown?
items.back: nohup launches a command immune to SIGHUP up front (logging to nohup.out). disown removes an already-running job from the shell's job table so it won't receive SIGHUP at shell exit. Both help a job survive logout.
items.hint: One is preemptive, one is after the fact.

items.front: How does the nice value affect scheduling?
items.back: Niceness ranges −20 (highest priority) to +19 (lowest), default 0. A HIGHER nice value means LOWER priority — you're being nicer to other processes. Set it with 'nice', change it with 'renice'.
items.hint: Higher number = more polite = less CPU.

items.front: What is ulimit used for?
items.back: It views and sets per-process resource limits (the getrlimit/setrlimit ceilings): max open files (-n), max user processes (-u), core size (-c), memory, etc. These are inherited across exec().
items.hint: Per-process resource ceilings in the shell.

items.prompt: You stopped a job with Ctrl-Z. Which command resumes it running in the background?
items.explanation: bg sends SIGCONT and resumes the job in the background. fg resumes it in the foreground, kill signals it, and disown just removes it from the job table.

items.prompt: A process has a nice value of +19. What does this imply?
choices: It has the highest possible scheduling priority
It has the lowest priority — it yields CPU to almost everything else
It can only be run by root
It is exempt from the OOM killer
items.explanation: +19 is the lowest priority. Higher nice values mean the process is 'nicer' and gives up CPU to others. Negative values (raising priority) require root.

items.prompt: A non-root user tries 'renice -n -5 -p 1234' and it fails. Why?
choices: renice only accepts positive values from anyone, but lowering nice (raising priority) requires root
items.explanation: Setting a negative nice value raises priority, which only root may do. Unprivileged users can only increase niceness (lower priority).

items.prompt: You set 'ulimit -n 4096' in your login shell. Which processes are affected?
choices: Every process the shell launches afterward, since limits are inherited across exec()
items.explanation: Resource limits are per-process and inherited by children across exec(), so the new cap applies to everything that shell launches — not system-wide and not retroactive.

items.prompt: What is the primary effect of running a command with nohup?
choices: It raises the command's scheduling priority
It makes the command ignore SIGHUP so it survives terminal/logout, and redirects output to nohup.out
It runs the command as root
It limits the command's memory usage
items.explanation: nohup makes the process immune to SIGHUP (the hangup sent when the controlling terminal closes), so it keeps running after logout, with output saved to nohup.out by default.
